title: Images and alternative text

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Images and alternative text; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Images and alternative text
sources.claim: Image alternative text communicates the image's purpose in context, while decorative images use an empty alt so they are ignored.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Images and alternative text
sources.claim: Image alternative text communicates the image's purpose in context, while decorative images use an empty alt so they are ignored.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Image alternative text communicates the image's purpose in context, while decorative images use an empty alt so they are ignored.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A chart image receives concise alt identifying its conclusion and a nearby detailed data table. Your goal is to choose and justify alternative text for informative, functional and decorative images. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Relative and absolute URLs; Paragraph and inline meaning. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Image alternative text communicates the image's purpose in context, while decorative images use an empty alt so they are ignored. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Alt text depends on surrounding content; filenames, image of prefixes, and redundant captions are usually poor substitutes. This draft therefore rejects: Every image needs a long visual description in its alt attribute.

includes: Write purpose-appropriate alt text or mark decoration empty without duplicating adjacent content.
Choose and justify alternative text for informative, functional and decorative images.
excludes: Alt text depends on surrounding content; filenames, image of prefixes, and redundant captions are usually poor substitutes.

outcomes: Choose and justify alternative text for informative, functional and decorative images.

examples.title: Images and alternative text: concrete case
examples.situation: A chart image receives concise alt identifying its conclusion and a nearby detailed data table.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A chart image receives concise alt identifying its conclusion and a nearby detailed data table.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Write purpose-based alt text for informative images and alt="" for images that are purely decorative.
examples.result: The result follows because Write purpose-based alt text for informative images and alt="" for images that are purely decorative.

examples.title: Images and alternative text: boundary case
examples.situation: Alt text depends on surrounding content; filenames, image of prefixes, and redundant captions are usually poor substitutes.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Alt text depends on surrounding content; filenames, image of prefixes, and redundant captions are usually poor substitutes.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Every image needs a long visual description in its alt attribute.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Write purpose-based alt text for informative images and alt="" for images that are purely decorative.
